source_metadata.title_raw: Christopher J. Diaz - A-18-015 - February 23, 2018 - Walnut Creek

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 23, 2018

Christopher J. Diaz
Milpitas City Attorney
Best Best & Krieger LLP
2001 N. Main Street, Suite 390
Walnut Creek, CA 94596

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-015

Dear Mr. Diaz:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Milpitas City Councilmember
Garry Barbadillo regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete
and accurate.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in governmental decisions
relating to the potential amendment of various specific plans and zoning ordinances to require more
service-type commercial uses to be included on the ground floor of new mixed-use residential
construction along Main Street given that the Councilmember has a leasehold interest in shared
commercial office space nearby Main Street that the Councilmember uses for his law firm?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions
because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
Councilmember's financial interests based on the facts presented.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-015
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are the City Attorney for the City of Milnitas and the authorized representative of City
Councilmember Garry Barbadillo. The Councilmember currently holds a five-year lease with others
for shared commercial office space, which the Councilmember uses for his law firm, the Law
Offices of Garry Barbadillo. The shared commercial office space is located at 16 Corning Avenue,
approximately 100 feet from Main Street. The Councilmember paid a $600 deposit and pays $600
per month under the shared lease, which has a total lease payment of $2,400 per month, and he
holds the right to sublease the office space under the terms of the shared lease. The Councilmember
owns and has invested more than $2,000 in his law firm.

With the current economic boom in Silicon Valley, the City has recently received a large
number of development proposals for high-density residential projects. Some of these proposed
projects are located on Main Street. Main Street currently contains a variety of commercial uses
along with high-density residential housing projects that have been approved in recent years.

Main Street is located within the areas of two of the City's specific plans. The majority of
Main Street is within the Midtown Specific Plan area, and a separate portion lies within the Transit
Area Specific Plan area. Each of these specific plans regulates the types of uses allowed and
imposes specific requirements for new construction within its respective area. For example, the
Midtown Specific Plan currently encourages, but does not require, commercial uses on the ground
floor of new mixed-use buildings.

The Midtown Specific Plan is currently undergoing a major update. Decisions to be
considered include whether the City should amend various specific plans and zoning ordinances,
including the Midtown Specific Plan, to require the ground floor of new mixed-use residential
construction along Main Street to include a requisite amount of square footage dedicated to service-
type commercial uses. You have asked whether the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit the
Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the potential inclusion of this new
requirement in these various specific plans and zoning ordinances.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision that would
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial
interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The Act identifies an official's financial interests that may
give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest in Section 87103. Of those interests, the
Councilmember's interests involved in the decisions at issue are the following:

• Commercial Office Space: Section 87103(b) provides that an official has an interest in any real
property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. The
Councilmember pays $7,200 per year in rent for the office space used by his law firm under the
shared lease. Therefore, the Councilmember has a leasehold interest in his law firm's
commercial office space.

File No. A-18-015
Page No. 3

• Law Firm: Section 87103(a) provides that an official has an interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. Because the Councilmember has invested more than $2,000 in his law firm, he has a business interest in the law firm.²

• Personal Finances: An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members under Section 87103.

Thus, with respect to the decisions at issue, the Councilmember has a leasehold interest in his law firm's commercial office space, a business interest in his law firm, and an interest in his personal finances and those of immediate family members.³

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial effect is named and a party in, or the subject, of a governmental decision. For a financial party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision at issue, the financial effect of that decision on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).)

Commercial Office Space

Regulation 18702.2(b), which sets forth the Act's materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's leasehold interest in real property, provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's leasehold is material if the decision will:

(1) Change the termination date of the lease;
(2) Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property;
(3) Increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the official has a right to sublease the property;
(4) Change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property;
(5) Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property.

Based on the facts presented, the potential changes to the specific plans and zoning ordinances at issue would not affect the shared lease or the underlying real property. Therefore, the effect of those decisions on the Councilmember's leasehold interest in his law firm's commercial office space is not material.

² The Councilmember also has a potentially disqualifying interest in any client of his firm if he has received income of $500 or more from the client in the 12 months prior to the decision. However, no clients of the firm have been identified so we are unable to analyze disqualification based upon the Councilmember's interests in any particular client. Accordingly, we must note that the conclusion reached in this letter is applicable only to the extent that the decisions do not potentially affect a client of the law firm. We refer you to Regulation 18702.3 for the applicable materiality standards for financial interests in sources of income.

³ Because a decision's financial effect on an official's personal finances or those of immediate family members is not considered separately if the decision at issue also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation 18702.5(c)), we do not further analyze the Councilmember's interest in his personal finances.

File No. A-18-015
Page No. 4

Law Firm

18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest in a business entity not explicitly involved in a decision is material if "a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in . . . the value of the a privately held business interest." There is no indication from the facts presented that the potential changes to the specific plans and zoning ordinances at issue would contribute to a change in the value of the Councilmember's law firm. Therefore, the effect of those decisions on the Councilmember's business interest in his law firm is not material.

Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the potential amendment of various specific plans and zoning ordinances to require more service-type commercial uses on the ground floor of new mixed-use residential construction along Main Street because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial interests based on the facts presented.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl
